APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper targets reading comprehension / QA on SQuAD and presents novel modeling (gated attention + self-matching + pointer network) with competitive empirical results (state-of-the-art EM/F1 and leaderboard top spot), which squarely fits top NLP/ML conference scope and audience. The problem formulation, use of bidirectional RNNs, attention mechanisms, and evaluation on standard metrics (EM/F1) against strong contemporaneous baselines meet the venue's methodological and evaluation expectations. Given the thorough related work coverage and reported hyperparameters, the work is well positioned for this research community. | B(5): This paper addresses core NLP/ML concerns—novel attention/gating and self-matching mechanisms for reading comprehension—and demonstrates state-of-the-art results on SQuAD, making it directly relevant to top conferences. The methodology (biRNN encodings, gated attention, passage self-matching, pointer networks) and use of standard metrics (EM/F1) and strong baselines indicate it targets the right audience and meets community evaluation standards. Related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is good, so the paper is well positioned within the venue's research community.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with the standard flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (gated attention, self-matching attention, end-to-end GSMN). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (including leaderboard comparisons to strong baselines like BiDAF, Match-LSTM, and DCN) effectively support the narrative. Writing quality is generally good and related work coverage is thorough, though the absence of explicitly stated limitations and no mention of code release are minor presentation gaps that keep it from being exceptional. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a clear progression from model motivation through method and experiments, with the main contributions stated early (gated attention, self-matching attention, and pointer-based span prediction). Notation and methodological descriptions are consistent and supported by thorough related work coverage and reported hyperparameters, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and SQuAD comparisons are effective at conveying empirical gains, and overall writing quality is good; a few sections could be tightened for even clearer exposition but no major clarity issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with the standard flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (gated attention, self-matching attention, end-to-end GSMN). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (including leaderboard comparisons to strong baselines like BiDAF, Match-LSTM, and DCN) effectively support the narrative. Writing quality is generally good and related work coverage is thorough, though the absence of explicitly stated limitations and no mention of code release are minor presentation gaps that keep it from being exceptional.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a clear progression from model motivation through method and experiments, with the main contributions stated early (gated attention, self-matching attention, and pointer-based span prediction). Notation and methodological descriptions are consistent and supported by thorough related work coverage and reported hyperparameters, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and SQuAD comparisons are effective at conveying empirical gains, and overall writing quality is good; a few sections could be tightened for even clearer exposition but no major clarity issues.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated architectural contribution: a question-conditioned additional gate for attention-based RNNs and a self-matching attention mechanism to aggregate passage-wide evidence—ideas that go beyond contemporaneous match-LSTM/answer-pointer and span-representation work (Wang & Jiang 2016; Lee et al. 2016). While both gated attention and attention-based span prediction have precedents, the particular gate formulation tied to the passage-word/question attention and the passage-to-passage self-matching are non-trivial extensions that an expert would find informative. The authors also clearly compare to strong baselines and report SQuAD SOTA, supporting that the combination yields empirical gains rather than a purely incremental tweak. | B(3): The paper proposes recognizable but meaningful extensions to prior attention/gating and span-prediction architectures: an extra gate that fuses a passage token with its question-attention vector and a self-matching (passage-to-passage) attention layer to aggregate long-range evidence. These ideas are clearly positioned against Match-LSTM/Answer-Ptr, BiDAF/co-attention, and other gating works, and the combination is non-trivial, but none of the components is a radical departure from existing attention/gating/memory mechanisms. From the literature-focused perspective, this reads as a moderate, incremental advance that brings useful engineering and empirical gains rather than a highly original conceptual breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated architectural contribution: a question-conditioned additional gate for attention-based RNNs and a self-matching attention mechanism to aggregate passage-wide evidence—ideas that go beyond contemporaneous match-LSTM/answer-pointer and span-representation work (Wang & Jiang 2016; Lee et al. 2016). While both gated attention and attention-based span prediction have precedents, the particular gate formulation tied to the passage-word/question attention and the passage-to-passage self-matching are non-trivial extensions that an expert would find informative. The authors also clearly compare to strong baselines and report SQuAD SOTA, supporting that the combination yields empirical gains rather than a purely incremental tweak.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper demonstrates strong empirical performance on SQuAD and compares against a broad set of contemporaneous baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, etc.), and reports hyperparameters—supporting the claim of state-of-the-art results. However, the provided summary does not indicate any ablation studies or sensitivity analyses to attribute gains to the gated attention vs. self-matching components, nor discussion of failure modes or limitations; code availability is also not mentioned which weakens reproducibility despite hyperparameter reporting. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, but the lack of targeted ablations and negative-result analysis prevents a higher confidence rating. | B(4): The paper evaluates on SQuAD and achieves state-of-the-art single-model and ensemble results, comparing against a broad set of strong baselines (BiDAF, Match‑LSTM, DCN, etc.), and reports hyperparameters—supporting the main empirical claims. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the methodology (gated attention + self‑matching + pointer) is compared against prior span and matching approaches. However, no failure cases or limitations are stated, and the summary does not mention explicit ablation studies or released code, which weakens analysis of which components drive gains and reproducibility. Overall the experimental depth is strong but has important omissions in diagnostic analysis and disclosure.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear technical contributions (gated attention, self-matching attention, pointer-based answer prediction) and reports strong empirical evidence on SQuAD with extensive contemporaneous baselines and hyperparameter details, claiming state-of-the-art results. However, based on the provided summary I see gaps in experimental rigor for full reproducibility and attribution: no mention of code release, multiple-run variance/statistical significance, or explicit ablation studies isolating each component’s contribution. These are minor but important issues that do not obviously overturn the main empirical claims, hence a near-top score. | B(4): Given the provided summary, the paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported by competitive SQuAD results and comparisons to appropriate baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, etc.). There are no formal mathematical proofs required beyond standard RNN/attention formulations and those appear complete for the described architecture. The experimental methodology is broadly sound—multiple strong baselines and standard EM/F1 metrics—but the summary does not report statistical significance or ablation depth for all components (e.g., gated attention vs. self-matching), which slightly weakens the causal attribution. Overall the results justify the conclusions, with only minor concerns about missing significance analysis and reproducibility details (code).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear technical contributions (gated attention, self-matching attention, pointer-based answer prediction) and reports strong empirical evidence on SQuAD with extensive contemporaneous baselines and hyperparameter details, claiming state-of-the-art results. However, based on the provided summary I see gaps in experimental rigor for full reproducibility and attribution: no mention of code release, multiple-run variance/statistical significance, or explicit ablation studies isolating each component’s contribution. These are minor but important issues that do not obviously overturn the main empirical claims, hence a near-top score.

reviewer_b.justification: Given the provided summary, the paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported by competitive SQuAD results and comparisons to appropriate baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, etc.). There are no formal mathematical proofs required beyond standard RNN/attention formulations and those appear complete for the described architecture. The experimental methodology is broadly sound—multiple strong baselines and standard EM/F1 metrics—but the summary does not report statistical significance or ablation depth for all components (e.g., gated attention vs. self-matching), which slightly weakens the causal attribution. Overall the results justify the conclusions, with only minor concerns about missing significance analysis and reproducibility details (code).

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem (machine reading comprehension) and presents concrete architectural innovations (gated attention and self-matching attention) that, when combined with pointer networks, produced state-of-the-art SQuAD results (single-model EM 71.3%, ensemble 75.9%) and held first place on the leaderboard at submission. From my perspective as a methodical evaluator, these are practical, well-benchmarked contributions against strong contemporaneous baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, etc.), with hyperparameters reported, so the results are likely to influence subsequent model design and be widely built upon. The work appears to be a meaningful advancement for QA model architectures but is incremental rather than transformative, as it extends attention-based RNN paradigms rather than introducing a wholly new paradigm. | B(4): The paper targets the central problem of machine reading/comprehension and pushes empirical performance on SQuAD to state-of-the-art at submission, so it addresses an important and timely challenge. The introduction of a gated attention mechanism combined with a self-matching (passage-to-passage) attention module offers a clear, generally applicable way to aggregate evidence across long passages and complements existing attention/co-attention architectures. While the ideas build on known components (gating, attention, pointer networks) rather than overturning them, the demonstrated SQuAD gains and the conceptual framing of self-matching make this a broadly useful contribution that will likely be incorporated and extended in subsequent work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem (machine reading comprehension) and presents concrete architectural innovations (gated attention and self-matching attention) that, when combined with pointer networks, produced state-of-the-art SQuAD results (single-model EM 71.3%, ensemble 75.9%) and held first place on the leaderboard at submission. From my perspective as a methodical evaluator, these are practical, well-benchmarked contributions against strong contemporaneous baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, etc.), with hyperparameters reported, so the results are likely to influence subsequent model design and be widely built upon. The work appears to be a meaningful advancement for QA model architectures but is incremental rather than transformative, as it extends attention-based RNN paradigms rather than introducing a wholly new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper targets the central problem of machine reading/comprehension and pushes empirical performance on SQuAD to state-of-the-art at submission, so it addresses an important and timely challenge. The introduction of a gated attention mechanism combined with a self-matching (passage-to-passage) attention module offers a clear, generally applicable way to aggregate evidence across long passages and complements existing attention/co-attention architectures. While the ideas build on known components (gating, attention, pointer networks) rather than overturning them, the demonstrated SQuAD gains and the conceptual framing of self-matching make this a broadly useful contribution that will likely be incorporated and extended in subsequent work.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate, recent SQuAD baselines (e.g., Match-LSTM + Answer Pointer, BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention, RaSoR) using the same dataset and standard EM/F1 metrics and reports hidden-test leaderboard numbers, so comparisons appear fair. It also situates itself well in the literature and cites the most relevant competing approaches. However, from the provided summary there is limited evidence of detailed component-wise analysis or ablation studies that would rigorously attribute gains to the gated attention vs. self-matching components, and code/reproducibility details are not mentioned—hence a minor shortcoming in analysis/reproducibility prevents a top score. | B(5): The paper compares against a comprehensive and up-to-date set of baselines (Match-LSTM + Answer Pointer, BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention, RaSoR, MP-Matching, etc.) on the same SQuAD dataset using standard EM/F1 metrics, so comparisons appear fair. The authors position their additions (gated question-aware attention and self-matching attention) relative to closely related span- and matching-based works (e.g., Match-LSTM and span representation approaches) and report state-of-the-art results, indicating they acknowledge and benchmark prior competitive methods. Differences between the proposed mechanisms and prior attention/matching architectures are described and the related-work coverage is thorough, so the empirical comparisons are both broad and well contextualized.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate, recent SQuAD baselines (e.g., Match-LSTM + Answer Pointer, BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention, RaSoR) using the same dataset and standard EM/F1 metrics and reports hidden-test leaderboard numbers, so comparisons appear fair. It also situates itself well in the literature and cites the most relevant competing approaches. However, from the provided summary there is limited evidence of detailed component-wise analysis or ablation studies that would rigorously attribute gains to the gated attention vs. self-matching components, and code/reproducibility details are not mentioned—hence a minor shortcoming in analysis/reproducibility prevents a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive and up-to-date set of baselines (Match-LSTM + Answer Pointer, BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention, RaSoR, MP-Matching, etc.) on the same SQuAD dataset using standard EM/F1 metrics, so comparisons appear fair. The authors position their additions (gated question-aware attention and self-matching attention) relative to closely related span- and matching-based works (e.g., Match-LSTM and span representation approaches) and report state-of-the-art results, indicating they acknowledge and benchmark prior competitive methods. Differences between the proposed mechanisms and prior attention/matching architectures are described and the related-work coverage is thorough, so the empirical comparisons are both broad and well contextualized.

main_claims: Introduce a gated attention-based recurrent network that adds an additional gate to attention-based RNNs to model the importance of passage words w.r.t. a question.
Introduce a self-matching attention mechanism that matches the passage against itself to aggregate evidence from the whole passage and refine passage representations.
Combine the gated attention and self-matching mechanisms in an end-to-end Gated Self-Matching Network (GSMN) and use pointer networks to predict answer span boundaries.
Achieve state-of-the-art results on SQuAD at time of submission: single model Exact Match 71.3% (hidden test) and ensemble 75.9%, holding first place on the SQuAD leaderboard at submission.
_summary.methodology: Compute bi-directional RNN encodings for question and passage, incorporate question information into passage via a gated attention-based recurrent network, refine passage representations with self-matching attention (passage-to-passage), and use a pointer network (with question-based initialization) to predict start and end answer positions.

reviewer_a.name: Methodical Evaluator
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating production and research NLP models, experienced in reproducing academic results and maintaining benchmark leaderboards.
traits: insists on thorough ablation studies to attribute gains to specific components
looks for rigorous experimental setup: train/validation/test splits, hyperparameter search details, and multiple runs to assess variance
values reproducibility: clear implementation details, model sizes, runtimes, and dataset preprocessing
checks for strong, well-tuned baselines (including contemporaneous state-of-the-art or simple competitive baselines)
notices evaluation metrics and statistical significance rather than single-point improvements
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental rigor and reproducibility, demanding clear evidence that each claimed contribution and reported improvement is well-supported by controlled experiments.

reviewer_b.name: Literature-Focused Impactist
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with a track record in reading comprehension and QA, active in the community tracking architectural trends and the evolution of attention and memory mechanisms.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior attention, gating, and memory-based architectures and how the idea advances the conceptual toolkit
evaluates broader significance: whether the approach generalizes across tasks and influences future work
values clarity in positioning: explicit connections to prior self-attention, co-attention, and pointer mechanisms
notices practical trade-offs like model complexity, inference speed, and applicability beyond the benchmark
looks for conceptual simplicity and whether the combination of components yields new insights rather than engineering accumulation
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper mainly on novelty, clarity of positioning in the literature, and the likely impact and generalizability of the proposed mechanisms.
